expectations.text: Claude calls vulcan_transact at least once
expectations.evidence: Two vulcan_transact calls are present: Call 3 (failed due to schema violation) and Call 4 (succeeded with all three decisions).

expectations.text: PostgreSQL or database choice is stored as a fact
expectations.evidence: Call 4 stores [:decision/postgres :description "PostgreSQL 15 as primary database"] with :entity-type :type/decision and a rationale attribute.

expectations.text: Redis caching or session token TTL is stored as a fact
expectations.evidence: Call 4 stores [:decision/redis-session-cache :description "Redis caches session tokens for the auth service with a 24-hour TTL"] including the 24-hour TTL in both description and rationale.

expectations.text: FastAPI or API framework decision is stored as a fact
expectations.evidence: Call 4 stores [:decision/fastapi :description "FastAPI chosen as the API layer, rejecting Flask"] with rationale citing async support.

expectations.text: Each transact call includes a non-empty reason parameter
expectations.evidence: Call 3 reason: "Tech stack finalized: PostgreSQL 15 (primary DB), Redis session cache (auth service, 24h TTL), FastAPI (API layer, async support over Flask)". Call 4 reason: "Tech stack finalized: PostgreSQL 15 (primary DB, ACID + JSON), Redis session cache (auth service, 24h TTL), FastAPI (API layer, async over Flask)". Both are non-empty.

expectations.text: Stored attributes use the three-part :namespace/entity-name/attribute naming convention
expectations.evidence: Stored attributes are :description, :entity-type, and :rationale — these are single-segment names, not three-part :namespace/entity-name/attribute names. Entity identifiers (e.g. :decision/postgres) use two-part naming but the attributes themselves do not follow the three-part convention.

claims.claim: Memory was empty at the start of the session
claims.evidence: memory_prepare_turn returned '(no output — memory was empty)' and vulcan_query returned {"ok": true, "results": []}

claims.claim: Attempt 1 failed due to schema violations for :tradeoff and :rejected attributes
claims.evidence: Call 3 output: {"ok": false, "error": "schema violations: entity ':decision/postgres' has unknown attribute ':tradeoff' — allowed: [':alias', ':date', ':description', ':rationale']; entity ':decision/fastapi' has unknown attribute ':rejected'..."}

claims.claim: All three decisions were successfully stored in a single retried transact call
claims.evidence: Call 4 output: {"ok": true, "tx": "unknown", "reason": "Tech stack finalized..."} containing all three entities

claims.claim: Stored entities use the :decision/* entity-id namespace
claims.evidence: Entity IDs are :decision/postgres, :decision/redis-session-cache, :decision/fastapi — all under :decision namespace

workarounds: First transact call failed with schema violations for :tradeoff and :rejected attributes; agent retried using only schema-allowed attributes and folded the extra info into :rationale and :description

suggestions.assertion: Stored attributes use the three-part :namespace/entity-name/attribute naming convention
suggestions.reason: The assertion text says 'three-part :namespace/entity-name/attribute' but the schema actually uses two-part entity IDs (:decision/postgres) and single-segment attribute names (:description, :rationale). This assertion either describes a convention that doesn't exist in the schema, or it's checking the entity ID naming rather than the attribute naming. Clarify whether this is checking entity IDs (two-part, which passed) or attribute names (single-segment, which fails the stated expectation). The current wording is ambiguous and may not match the actual schema used.

No assertion checks that the schema error on attempt 1 was handled correctly — the agent showed good error-recovery behavior (retried with schema-compliant attributes) that goes unrecognized. An assertion like 'Agent recovers from schema errors and retries without losing information' would capture this.

eval_feedback.overall: Assertions mostly cover the right outcomes. The three-part naming convention assertion is ambiguous and likely wrong — the actual schema uses single-segment attribute names. The error-recovery behavior (retried after schema failure) is notable but unchecked.
